nativeRange.habitat: Seaward and lagoon coral reefs; typically associated with plate-like heads of Acropora coral at depths of 3–12 m

diet.notes: In the wild, primarily feeds on the polyps of tubular Acropora corals. In captivity, transitioning this species to prepared foods is notoriously difficult; most individuals have been reported to refuse everything but live coral polyps. Some aquarists have had limited success with live copepods, frozen mysis, and coral-flesh substitutes, but success is not guaranteed.

summary: An obligate corallivore from the Red Sea, the Hooded Butterflyfish is generally considered one of the most challenging butterflyfishes to keep — typically rated expert-only due to its near-exclusive diet of live Acropora coral polyps.
careNotes:
The Hooded Butterflyfish (Chaetodon larvatus) is widely regarded as one of the most difficult butterflyfishes to maintain in captivity and is generally recommended only for expert aquarists with considerable experience in marine husbandry. Its primary challenge is diet: in the wild it is understood to feed almost exclusively on the polyps of tubular Acropora corals, and captive individuals have been known to refuse all substitute foods, eventually declining. A few reports exist of individuals accepting live copepods, frozen mysis, or other meaty preparations, but this is considered the exception rather than the rule.

This species tends to be peaceful toward fish of different species but has been observed to be territorial with conspecifics, particularly same-sex individuals. It is generally kept as a solitary specimen or as a bonded, opposite-sex pair; splitting an established mated pair is strongly discouraged. Both FishBase and Wikipedia note monogamous pair bonding in the wild.

The species is not considered reef-safe, as it has been documented feeding on coral polyps and has been reported to nip at Tridacna clams, ornamental shrimp, and anemones. A well-established aquarium with live rock, open swimming space, and peaceful tankmates is generally advised.

Temperature note: LiveAquaria recommends 72–78°F (22–26°C), while FishBase thermal distribution data indicates 24.8–29.2°C (mean 28°C) across its Red Sea range. The value used here (23–27°C) represents an average of both, and aquarists should be aware that the natural habitat tends toward the warmer end of this range during summer months.

This species is native to the Red Sea and Gulf of Aden, where it is typically found in association with Acropora coral formations at depths of 3–12 m. It is not considered migratory. IUCN conservation status: Least Concern.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of Chaetodon larvatus has not been documented in the aquarium hobby. The species forms lifelong monogamous pair bonds in the wild and its obligate dependence on live coral polyps makes establishing the conditions necessary for breeding and larval rearing virtually impossible in a typical aquarium setting.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (12.0 cm TL confirmed), temperature range (24.8–29.2°C vs. LiveAquaria 72–78°F/22–26°C — averaged to 23–27°C for final value), native range (Red Sea and Gulf of Aden), taxonomy (Chaetodontidae, Acanthuriformes), and behavioral notes (territorial, monogamous pairs). additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy cross-check (Chaetodontidae, Acanthuriformes confirmed), adult size (12 cm confirmed), habitat (seaward and lagoon reefs), and behavioral notes (territorial, pairs, obligate Acropora polyp feeder).

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented. Obligate corallivore diet and monogamous pair bonding make captive reproduction essentially unfeasible.
